fact: August 25 sits exactly 237 days into the year - that's like counting every single day since New Year's and then adding 67 more!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many trees start secretly preparing for fall by slowly cutting off food to their leaves, even though they still look totally green.
Monarch butterflies are typically in the middle of their epic migration south right now, with some flying over 2,000 miles without stopping to ask for directions.
The sun rises about 2 minutes later than it did just a week ago - it's like the Earth is hitting the snooze button on summer.
This date falls in the middle of Virgo season, when the zodiac sign is associated with organization and attention to detail.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, August 25 is deep winter, which means kids there might be experiencing cold weather while the Northern Hemisphere sweats in summer heat.
Crickets chirp faster or slower based on temperature, and on warm August 25 evenings, you can actually count their chirps to figure out how hot it is outside.
The autumn equinox is still about a month away, but many animals in northern climates are already getting restless and starting to hoard food like tiny, furry doomsday preppers.
August 25 typically marks the time when spider webs become super visible in the morning dew - it's like nature's way of showing off all the creepy crawly architecture in your backyard.
In northern regions, the aurora borealis often starts becoming more active around this time as the nights get longer and darker - perfect for sky light shows.
Late August is when many flowers go to seed, shooting their babies out into the world like tiny plant cannons - some seeds can fly over 100 miles.
Ocean temperatures in many coastal areas reach their warmest point around August 25, even though the hottest air temperatures usually happened weeks ago - water is basically a slow learner.
